source: MLA Handbook 9th ed., 6.42: in-text citations of direct quotations
article: Fitzgerald writes that Gatsby 'believed in the green light, the orgastic future that year by year recedes before us.'

critiques.text_quote: Fitzgerald writes that Gatsby 'believed in the green light, the orgastic future that year by year recedes before us.'
critiques.question: In MLA 9th, 6.42: a direct quotation requires an in-text citation with a page number. The quote is present, the citation is not. This is the integrity-line move a grader is most likely to mark.
critiques.why_it_matters: An unattributed direct quotation is the easiest line to flag for plagiarism risk.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add the page reference. In MLA: (Fitzgerald 180). The Works Cited entry must list the edition you used.
critiques.replacement: Fitzgerald writes that Gatsby 'believed in the green light, the orgastic future that year by year recedes before us' (180).

source: APA Publication Manual 7th ed., section 8.13: page numbers on direct quotations
article: As Brown argues, the modern presidency was 'an institutional response to wartime crisis' (Brown, 2019).

critiques.question: APA 7th, section 8.13: a direct quotation requires the page number in the in-text citation. Your citation has author and year but no page. The reader cannot locate the quoted material in the source.
critiques.why_it_matters: Without the page, the citation does not let the reader verify the quote.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add the page reference: (Brown, 2019, p. 14). Use 'p.' for one page, 'pp.' for a range.

article: The historiography of the Reconstruction era was reshaped in the 1980s by a new generation of scholars who showed that the period was a serious, partly successful experiment in interracial democracy rolled back by violence and federal abandonment.

critiques.text_quote: The historiography of the Reconstruction era was reshaped in the 1980s by a new generation of scholars who showed that the period was a serious, partly successful experiment in interracial democracy rolled back by violence and federal abandonment.
critiques.question: This sentence is a close paraphrase of Eric Foner's argument in Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution (1988). Paraphrased ideas need citation as much as direct quotations do. Without it the sentence reads as the writer's original synthesis.
critiques.why_it_matters: Unattributed paraphrase of a specific scholar's argument is plagiarism in any style manual.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add the citation in the style your essay uses. In Chicago notes-bibliography: a footnote citing Foner, Reconstruction (1988), with page range for the relevant chapter. In MLA: (Foner 281-307).

source: MLA Handbook 9th ed.: works-cited entry required for every body citation
article: Recent scholarship has revisited the question of Reconstruction's economic legacy (Foner 281-307).

critiques.question: Your in-text citation (Foner 281-307) does not appear in the Works Cited list. MLA 9th: every in-text citation must correspond to an entry in Works Cited. A grader checking the back matter will find nothing for 'Foner.'
critiques.why_it_matters: An orphan in-text citation looks like the writer pulled the source name from a memory of having read it.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Add the entry. In MLA: 'Foner, Eric. Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution, 1863-1877. Harper Perennial, 2014.'

source: MLA Handbook 9th ed., 6.36: block quotations of prose
article: Morrison writes: 'It was not a story to pass on. They forgot her like a bad dream. After they made up their tales, shaped and decorated them, those that saw her on the porch quickly and deliberately forgot her.'

critiques.text_quote: 'It was not a story to pass on. They forgot her like a bad dream. After they made up their tales, shaped and decorated them, those that saw her on the porch quickly and deliberately forgot her.'
critiques.question: Six lines of prose set inline with quotation marks. MLA 9th, 6.36: prose quotations of four or more lines are set off as an indented block, half-inch indent, no surrounding quotation marks, with the parenthetical citation after the closing punctuation.
critiques.why_it_matters: An unblocked long quotation cluttesr the page and signals the writer has not consulted the style rules.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Reformat as a block: drop the quotation marks, indent half an inch from the left margin, place the citation after the closing period: 'forgot her. (Morrison 275)'

source: Style-consistency diagnostic: mixed styles within one essay
article: Earlier critics argued (Smith 14) that the novel rejected its own conclusion. More recent work has reframed the question (Jones, 2019).

critiques.question: Your first citation (Smith 14) is MLA. Your second (Jones, 2019) is APA. A single essay has to commit to one style; mixing them tells the grader the writer is not enforcing their own format.
critiques.why_it_matters: Mixed citation styles inside one essay is the surface signal that hits a grader's eye first.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Pick one. If MLA, change (Jones, 2019) to (Jones 41) with the page you cite, and rebuild the back matter as Works Cited. If APA, change (Smith 14) to (Smith, 2003, p. 14) and rebuild as References.

source: Chicago Manual of Style 17th ed., 14.20: full notes vs. shortened notes

critiques.question: This is the first reference to Foner in the essay. Chicago 17, 14.20: the first note for any source is a full citation (author, full title, publication facts, page); subsequent notes use the shortened form. Your first note is already shortened, which leaves the reader without publication facts.
critiques.why_it_matters: A reader checking the source cannot find the edition or publisher from a shortened note alone.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Replace with the full note: 'Eric Foner, Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution, 1863-1877 (New York: Harper Perennial, 2014), 281.' Use the shortened form for any later reference.
critiques.replacement: 1. Eric Foner, Reconstruction: America's Unfinished Revolution, 1863-1877 (New York: Harper Perennial, 2014), 281.

source: MLA Handbook 9th ed., 5.86 and 5.87: online sources
article: According to a recent New York Times analysis, federal disaster spending has tripled since 2000 (Tabuchi).

critiques.question: MLA 9th, 5.86 and 5.87: a Works Cited entry for an online article needs the title, the publication, the date, and the URL. Citing 'Tabuchi' alone without the locating information leaves the source unverifiable.
critiques.why_it_matters: An online source the reader cannot locate is, for grading purposes, an uncited claim.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Build the full Works Cited entry. MLA: 'Tabuchi, Hiroko. "Federal Disaster Spending Has Tripled Since 2000." The New York Times, 14 March 2024, www.nytimes.com/2024/03/14/climate/federal-disaster-spending.html.'
